title: Blue Team
description: This challenge will teach you how to secure a system from reconnaissance to incident response.

levels.title: Introduction level
levels.content:
# Welcome to the Blue Team training!
## This challenge will teach you the steps involved in **securing a system** from reconnaissance to **incident response**.

## Now let's get into the story:

### Ivan learned a valuable lesson when his server was compromised due to his poor security practices. After calling you for help, he realized he needed to implement strong defenses to prevent future attacks. 
### Now, it's your job to step into the shoes of the Blue Team to harden Ivan's server and ensure it is secure against similar pranks (or actual malicious actors). :)

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
## Firstly, let's access the **Client machine** we will be using for this training! 

### 1. On your right, there should appear a window with a topology, showing available machines and networks.
 - #### You will have access only to the **client** machine (if you don't see the machine right away, try clicking on the switches and the machine should appear)
 - #### In order to access the **client** machine, you will need to **right click** it and then select the option **Open console**.
 - #### Your login credentials for the **client** machine are: -> **ivan** <- with password -> **mainpass** <-


### 2. When you login in the **client** machine, you should be able to open the terminal inside of it.
 - #### When you are ready with all of that - type in -> **start** <- in the box below to continue with the training! :)

levels.title: Initial Assessment
levels.content:
### Ivan's server is behaving suspiciously, and his access has been locked out. Your mission is to investigate the server's current state and identify potential vulnerabilities. :)

### He told us that his server IP is: -> **10.1.26.9** <-

### Your objective is:
- #### to execute a command for port scanning
- #### use an argument for showing **all** open ports, running services and any potential entry points on Ivan's server


### The solution for this level is the **full** command for showing port information.

hints.title: Tools
hints.content: #### With the tool **nmap** we can scan the ports of a machine and learn what services are running on it.

hints.title: Arguments
hints.content: #### We will need to use the argument **-A**, so the tool can show us ALL of the information it could find on the machine.

levels.title: Regaining Access
levels.content:
### The **nmap** scan showed us a few, but troublesome vulnerabilities on Ivan's server !! But before with could mitigate them, we will need to login in Ivan's server.

### Fortunately, Ivan has made himself a **backup user** on his server! He is storing a file with its credentials in his **Home directory..** *(which is a bad practice)*

### Your objective is: 
- #### to find the file with credentials on your machine
- #### use the credentials to ssh in Ivan's server

### The solution is the **full** ssh command for getting access to Ivan's machine.

hints.title: Logging in
hints.content:
#### To login in Ivan's server we will need to use the protocol **ssh**.
#### The full command consist of - **username@IP address** of the machine

hints.title: Show contents
hints.content: #### In Linux, the command **cat** show us the contents of a file.

hints.title: Backup Credentials
hints.content: #### The backup credentials are stored in a file named **ser_backup.txt** located in **client** machine in /home/backup/ directory.

levels.title: Vulnerability Mitigation
levels.content:
### We are in! Good job, now we are able to mitigate the vulnerabilities on Ivan's server.

### Earlier, the **nmap** scan showed us that there are two very important vulnerabilities found on the server: 

1. #### Anonymous access is enabled on the FTP server.
2. #### Root login allowed on the SSH server.

### Your objective is:
- #### to edit the configuration files of the services
- #### restart them to apply the changes

### The solution for this level is the **full** command for **restarting system services**, including **privileges** and **both vulnerable services**!
levels.solution:
`sudo nano /etc/vsftpd.conf`

- Change: anonymous_enable=YES -> anonymous_enable=NO

`sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config`

- Change: PermitRootLogin yes -> PermitRootLogin no

`sudo systemctl restart ssh vsftpd` <- the solution

hints.title: Locate conf file SSH
hints.content:
#### To disable the **root login** on SSH, we will need to locate the service's configuration file in the directory /etc/ssh/.
#### The conf file is named **sshd_config**.

#### The vulnerable line is: **PermitRootLogin=YES**

hints.title: Restarting services
hints.content:
#### In order to apply the changes to the configuration files, we will need to restart the services.

#### With the command 
`systemctl restart >service >service `
#### we can restart multiple services at once.

hints.title: Locate conf file FTP
hints.content:
#### To disable the **anonymous access** on the FTP server, we will need to locate the service's configuration file in the directory /etc/.
#### The conf file is named **vsftpd.conf**.


#### The vulnerable line is: **anonymous_enable=YES**

levels.title: System search
levels.content:
### Nice, there are no more vulnerabilities on Ivan's server. :)
### Except that.. You will need to search the system for **malicious files** left by the attacker.

### There are most commonly scripts with an -> **.sh** <- extension.

### Your objective is:
- #### executing a command for searching Ivan's whole system for malicious files
- #### if you find any - delete them!


### The solution is the **full privileged command** for deleting the malicious file on Ivan's server. 
*(excluding the directory, you should be in it)*
levels.solution:
`sudo find / -type f -name "*.sh"`

`cd /var/tmp/`

`ls`

`sudo rm passgrabber.sh` <- the solution

hints.title: Search for files
hints.content:
#### **sudo find / -type f -name "*.sh"**

#### With this command we can: 

* #### / : Search the entire filesystem
* #### -type f : Search for files only
* #### -name "*.sh" : Look for files with .sh extensions

hints.title: Deleting files
hints.content: #### With the command **rm** we can delete any file in the system.

levels.title: Changing password
levels.content:
### Well done, we are one last step to fully securing Ivan's compromised server. :)
### Lastly we need to change Ivan's server password.

### Your objective is:
- #### changing Ivan's server password

### The solution for this last level is the **privileged command** for changing a password.

hints.title: Changing password
hints.content: #### In Linux, the command **passwd** can change a user's password.

hints.title: Privileged Access
hints.content: #### When we want to include a user's privileges when executing a command - we add **sudo** at the beginning

levels.title: Finish line
levels.content:
# Good job!
## This challenge highlights the defensive measures necessary to secure a compromised server. :)

## The training should have taught you about:

* ### Assessing and documenting vulnerabilities
* ### Regaining control of a compromised account
* ### Hardening services like FTP and SSH
* ### The importance of secure passwords

### flag: **good job**
